strategy: Prevent aberrant cytoplasmic aggregation or restore nuclear import by modulating RNA-binding domain conformation
notes: RRM1 (residues 106-176) and RRM2 (residues 191-259) are the structured core of TDP-43. Small molecules binding RRM may stabilize nuclear conformation or prevent aggregation-prone misfolding. Challenge: RRM domains are highly conserved RNA-binding folds — selectivity is critical.

strategy: Prevent liquid-to-solid phase transition and amyloid-like fibril formation in the intrinsically disordered C-terminal region
notes: The CTD harbors most ALS-linked TARDBP mutations (A315T, M337V, Q331K, G348C). IDR is responsible for LLPS and aggregation. No high-resolution structure — relies on cryo-EM fibril structures and AlphaFold. Drug design for IDRs is frontier territory but high-impact.

druggable_site: Cryptic exon splice acceptor/donor sites in UNC13A intron 20-21
strategy: Steric-blocking ASO or small-molecule splicing modifier to prevent cryptic exon inclusion when TDP-43 is depleted
notes: RNA-targeted approach. Risk SNP rs12973192 modifies splice site strength. ASO approach validated preclinically (Brown 2022). Small-molecule splicing modifiers (inspired by risdiplam/SMA) may offer oral alternative to intrathecal ASO. High therapeutic relevance for sporadic ALS.

druggable_site: Cryptic exon splice site in STMN2 intron 1
strategy: Steric-blocking ASO or small-molecule splicing modifier to prevent cryptic exon inclusion and restore full-length STMN2
notes: STMN2 cryptic exon in intron 1 produces truncated non-functional transcript. ASO blocking validated preclinically. Oral splicing modifier would be transformative. STMN2 restoration directly addresses axonal regeneration failure in ALS.

strategy: Agonist binding to stabilize sigma-1R at MAM, enhancing ER-mitochondria tethering and calcium homeostasis
notes: Crystal structure solved 2016 (Schmidt et al, Nature). Trimeric protein with single transmembrane helix per monomer. Binding pocket well-characterized. Pridopidine is selective S1R agonist in Phase 3 (PREVAiLS). Other agonists (cutamesine, SA4503) also known. Structure enables rational optimization.

druggable_site: Dimer interface and aggregation-prone beta-barrel edge strands
strategy: Stabilize native SOD1 dimer to prevent monomer dissociation, misfolding, and aggregation
notes: SOD1 crystal structures extensively characterized. Mutant SOD1 aggregation proceeds through monomer dissociation → misfolding → oligomerization → fibrillization. Small molecules stabilizing the native dimer (like isoproterenol) shown to reduce aggregation in vitro. ASO/gene therapy approaches now clinical — small molecule may complement.

druggable_site: DYRK3, CK2, MARK4 kinase domains (regulate SG assembly/disassembly balance)
strategy: Modulate stress granule dynamics to prevent pathological persistence and maturation to solid aggregates
notes: SG assembly is kinase-regulated. DYRK3 is a key SG dissolution factor (Wippich 2013). CK2 phosphorylates multiple SG proteins. MARK4 regulates tau/cytoskeleton. Preventing SG persistence could block the SG→aggregate pathway. Challenge: SGs are normal stress responses — must tune, not abolish.

druggable_site: Allosteric sites on EAAT2 transporter or transcriptional enhancer elements
strategy: Upregulate EAAT2 expression or enhance transport activity to restore synaptic glutamate clearance
notes: EAAT2 loss is consistent finding in ALS tissue. Ceftriaxone (beta-lactam antibiotic) upregulates EAAT2 via NF-kB pathway — tested in ALS Phase 3 but failed (possibly due to insufficient CNS levels or ALS being too far downstream). Cryo-EM structure (2021) enables structure-based design of direct EAAT2 enhancers.

druggable_site: FRB domain (rapamycin binding) and FKBP12-rapamycin interface
strategy: Selective mTORC1 inhibition to activate autophagy without mTORC2-mediated metabolic side effects
notes: Rapamycin (via FKBP12) allosterically inhibits mTORC1, activating autophagy. mTORC1-selective inhibition preferred over ATP-competitive inhibitors (which hit mTORC2). Third-generation rapalogs and bi-steric inhibitors under development. Low-dose rapamycin being tested in ALS (RAP-ALS trial).

strategy: Modulate microglial proliferation and activation state by CSF1R kinase inhibition to reduce non-cell-autonomous motor neuron toxicity
notes: CSF1R drives microglial proliferation and inflammatory activation. Masitinib (CSF1R/KIT inhibitor) in Phase 3 for ALS. PLX5622 (selective CSF1R inhibitor) depletes microglia in preclinical models. Challenge: microglia have neuroprotective roles early — timing and selectivity matter. Non-depleting CSF1R modulators may be safer than full inhibitors.
